sentences.text: Yes.
To clarify, this woman who saved my life at that point-- there were also Polish Jews-- inmates-- who had also been shipped out of the Lodz ghetto.
We did volunteer for the transport.
We were all selected.
They were determined.
If I didn't make it, they would get out of it somehow.
Don't ask me how.
We made it-- one more cattle train, same scenario; again, many days and nights on the train; again, no food, no water, no bathroom facilities.
Eventually, the train stopped.
Juden [GERMAN].
And we were in a forest.
We walked through these woods.
It sounds so strange, but-- could breathe air-- after Auschwitz, and the constant smoke in the air, and the smell of burning flesh.
I didn't know what it was at first.
But you saw that smokestack all day long.
There was an odor in Auschwitz.
It smelled so good and clean.
We were marched for or a few kilometers.
I don't know how many-- two, three-- came to this enclosure.
And there were tents-- a few, not very many-- two, three-- huge canvas tents with clean straw on the ground.
And a young woman met us.
She was our kapo.
And she was nice.
She was a Polish Jew.
And she said we had arrived.
We were the second transport into a camp known as Bergen-Belsen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, the conditions were the same.
We still stood to be counted.
We still had the same quality and amount of food, but we could move around freely.
The first thing our little band did is we combined all of our straw and one blanket per person, used-- made one communal bed.
There wasn't that much space to lay on the ground, but we combined them so that we had a couple-- three blankets under us, three over us.
And we used to sleep like sardines, curled into each other for body warmth.
It was gettin' very cold.
I don't know which one of them decided.
I was the baby.
I was 14.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The oldest were a woman by the name of Sala and Hinda.
They were both 38.
They were the communist leaders.
Then there was a girl I became very close to, that I adored.
Her name was Basha.
She was 18.
Those three were the leaders, especially Hinda and Basha, more so than Sala.
We marched right under the lookouts' noses, singing songs in Polish that were quite revolutionary-- made us feel good-- to take a daily shower.
Now, mind you, it was just a pipe attached to a fence with a sieve [LAUGHS] on the end.
It was freezing, ice-cold out there.
The ground was hard.
If it thawed, we had muck.
We'd strip off our clothes-- our dress.
I mean, there was no other clothing-- and stand under that water and wash ourselves to prevent disease.
Because by then, the lice had come with everybody from Auschwitz.
Well, that's the way life went on in Bergen-Belsen.
Until one day, we went through a selection for a transport again, this time into Germany.
Now, the difference between Bergen-Belsen and Auschwitz was that Bergen-Belsen was supposed to be the selection place for labor camps.
There were no ovens, no gas chambers.
And one more time, we were-- all of us, our whole group, was selected for a labor transport into Germany-- cattle trains, true.
The ride wasn't quite as long-- maybe two-- two days and a night or two nights.
We were marched in the middle of the night, pouring mud.
And I tripped on something, twisted my ankle, and something lodged in my ankle-- very deep.
I didn't dare even falter.
I kept limping but going.
I had to keep up the pace.
When we got into the cattle car for that, I was ready to pass out.
One of my friends held my leg, another one-- and they let me spread it across them-- with all her might, pulled this thing out.
And it was a rusty, one-inch-long screw.
It was quite thick.
I had a scar for years.
I still have a dimple there.

When the other people on the train realized that I was injured-- and my leg, of course, was throbbing, swelling, pussy-- they proceeded to kick it and hit it-- so that we were all becoming animals-- so that I'd pull it back and they'd have the space.
Well, we did arrive in Magdeburg.
My ankle was about triple its size, full of pus.
We were marched to our barracks in the labor camp.
It was a room with windows; triple bunks, but space on it; clean straw.
And it was actually warm compared to what we were used to-- still freezing cold, but at least the wind didn't howl through it.
And on the next day, a woman-- a tall, austere-looking German female-- came to select for whatever it was she was selecting for.
And she picked 10 of us.
And we all noticed the same thing and were terrified.
We were the smallest, the scrawniest, the sickest-looking in the group.
And she marched us at full pace to work.
It turned out it was an SS kitchen in a factory-- at least I believe it was a factory.
She marched us in past all these security doors, took us to a closet-like room-- no windows.
There were buckets of potatoes and water, peeling knives, and little, tiny, like, milking stools.
We were told that was our job.
We were to peel potatoes for the SS kitchen.
She looked-- I mean, I was terrified of her.
We all were.
We were sure she was taking us to an extermination camp-- locked the door behind her.
A few hours later, she came back, and she said, shh.
She had a  big bowl of hot soup for us.
She told us not to eat the potato peels, because they really watched closely as to how much was missing.
She could have been killed for that.

Eventually, as she sternly marched us back and forth to our labor camp, she proceeded to tell us the reason she selected us-- because we looked so young, and we were the ones in need of saving more than the others.
She wanted to give us a chance.
One day, as we are marching-- hup 2, 3, 4 [NON-ENGLISH]-- she isn't taking us to our camp.
She took us to her apartment.
She decided we needed a warm shower or bath.
She even bought us raincoats-- little-- nothing-- little plastic, vinyl-- whatever.
Well, my utopia lasted three weeks.
They had taken chest x-rays, because we were working for the Germans.
Mine came back TB.
So I was told I was going to the hospital.
Well, I knew what that meant.
I was taken to a hospital-- kept in isolation with five other women.
I don't know who they were-- a Hungarian, a gypsy, a Russian, me.
And eventually, this time I was escorted by a German female soldier-- transported back to Bergen-Belsen.
Evidently, they didn't have transports going to from Magdeburg.
So we had to go on a passenger train under escort.
When I arrived in Bergen-Belsen, it had changed totally.
Now there were the three-tiered bunkers-- hundreds of thousands.
I don't know how many people-- disease-- in just four weeks time-- three, four weeks-- maybe it was longer.
I don't know.
It was the middle of the winter.
Dead bodies-- they'd collect the bodies and throw them outside-- the inmates had to.
They were like haystacks-- as high as you could throw them-- with the limbs, and the open eyes, and the sockets staring-- skeletons.
And people just kept dying, and dying, and dying, and dying, and dysentery, typhus.
There were so many of us dying.
And I guess, as spring approached, the allies were approaching-- I don't know.
I can't tell you the reason.
We were being shuffled from one building to another.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Eventually, I landed in my very last place.
I guess it was the typhus room.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had become friendly on the train back to Bergen-Belsen with another Polish Jewess.
And she was fortunate.
She was a seamstress.
And one day, she lifted her hand when they were looking for seamstresses.
So the SS woman officer took her to her home and had her sewing for her all day.
She also gave her some food.
In return for my watching this woman's piece of bread from the morning, and her tin can, and whatever, she would give me one or two bites of her bread.
So that was our alliance.
We became friendly, but not close friends.
Anyway, as we were moved from this one barrack to another, she and some others dragged me, because I couldn't walk by then-- planted me in a corner by two 20-ish females and their mother.
And the mother kept screaming, and screaming, and screaming, and-- the daughters kept yelling at her.
And what I remember, as improbable as it sounds, is why don't you die, mom?
Let go.
Mother, please-- mama, die.
And they'd say to each other, isn't she ever going to die?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
It was so foreign to me, you know?
I would have given anything for my mother.
And then at one point, they picked her up and tried to roll her on her side.
And I looked.
She started screaming.
It was so inhuman, like a banshee.
Her intestines, everything, was coming out.
It was out of her rectum, laying underneath her in just this pool of blood.
I don't know how that's physically possible.
I have no idea.
And I passed out again.
When I regained consciousness, the screams were continuing.
I couldn't stand it.
So I crawled on all fours between these bodies.
By then we probably had 12 inches of space per person-- no food, no water-- because nobody would come near us.
We were the typhus area.
And I tried to get this other Polish Jewess to let me in where she was.
She and a group of healthy ones had confiscated what must have been a men's-- SS German officers' men's restroom in this building.
And they wouldn't let me in.
They were sleeping in a bathtub.
They told me it's too cold.
It was a ceramic tile floor.
They told me I wouldn't live there.
It was that cold.
And also, they didn't want my typhus.
I can understand that.
So I somehow or other made it back, but I would not go to that same spot.
So I crawled till I found a spot.
I laid down.
We were in rows-- maybe 14 inches by 4 feet-- maybe a foot pathway.
And then, again, I found a spot.
I fell down-- lost consciousness.
And I don't know.
There is no way that I can tell you whether it was a day, a week, or a month.
It must have been quite a while.
I mean, typhus doesn't break like that.
But I remember all of a sudden floating up, and-- and it was a strange feeling.
Like, I thought, oh, oh, I'm awake.
I know what's happening.
And there was something very heavy.
I was face down on-- in this ground, on this whole left side.
And my arm was numb.
And it was pinned down.
And I couldn't pull it out.
I was too weak to lift myself.
But I somehow managed-- tried to push her off.
I realized it was another woman.
And I couldn't.
So I tugged, and I pulled.
And at last I released myself.
I reached out to give her a good shove, touched her, and I realized she was ice-cold and stiff.
This woman was obviously dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Janice, I was so dehumanized by then that I have recollection of one thought-- hallelujah, she's dead.
Now I can sprawl on top of her.
I can have room to lay down.
I did.
One whole part of me-- I just threw my arm out, and at least 1/2 of my body was on her.
And I promptly either went into a trance-like sleep or became unconscious again.
I don't know.
The next thing that I remember are voices, and everybody is screaming-- the Germans have left during the night.
The lookout towers are deserted.
And everyone who could was trying to squeeze, get out from this-- through this little opening in the door.
I mean, the rooms were-- these huts were very long, with one door into them in the center.
I don't know how to describe it.
I-- I have done a number of paintings of my recollections of the war.
That's the way I see them.
This time, I couldn't even lift my head up-- barely.
So I clawed the ground, and pulled and pulled, till I made it through the opening.
And then about 20 feet away, which might as well have been 20 miles, I saw a circular-- like a cistern-- a well-- with cobblestones surrounding it-- raised.
And there was a waterspout on the end.
And I made it all the way.
And I had my first drop of water.
I've heard that some of us were left like that for three to four weeks.
So it's quite possible I had been in that condition for that long.
Had a drop of water, pulled myself up, leaned against these stones, and watched an incredible scene.
The men from the men's side of Bergen-Belsen-- I didn't even know there were men there-- had cut through the wires.
Evidently, the electricity must have been turned off.
And they came pouring into the women's side.
Here were these skeletons.
And everybody there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --was it-- it was like a litany.
They were asking.
They were begging.
They were screaming out the names.
I made it back.
I don't know how.
I don't know when-- into that building.
I did not return to the dead woman's place-- found another spot-- passed out.
The girl who had become the German SS officer's seamstress came and saw me afterwards, and she brought me a green onion-- one little, tiny onion.
And the gal next to me tried to grab it from me.
You know, I almost clawed her eyes out.
It was my onion.
It was the first green vegetable I had seen in four years.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I ate it.
I moved.
I wasn't about to be near her-- crawled to another place, same scenario; have a very vague recollection of people, soldiers, handing me some food to eat.
I don't-- I-- it's so vague, I can't tell you.
I-- I think maybe I'd eat two bites and pass out, and-- I don't know.
The next thing I remember clearly is I was being lifted onto a stretcher.
Let me tell you that I come to [NON-ENGLISH].
I started screaming.
I was so scared.
And there were voices saying, it's OK, little one.
You're safe now.
We're taking you to a hospital.
That's all I needed to hear-- was hospital.
Don't lie to me about taking me to a hospital.
I don't need a hospital.
I can work.
I will work.
Please, please, please, let me go.
Don't take me to the hospital.
You see, everybody who was ever taken to a hospital was killed.
And they kept reassuring me.
And I didn't believe them.
I just kept begging.
Well, obviously, I fainted again-- lost consciousness.
I came to, and I felt something underneath me-- soft, somewhat.
And then I realized that something was covering me.
I opened my eyes, and everything was white.
I was laying on something white.
Something white was covering me.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --and kind of a floating view of an angel sitting there, all in white come in.
And I said, OK.
I didn't make it.
I guess it's OK.
I'm in heaven.
I've died.
This has to be heaven.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Lost consciousness again-- I was in the Red Cross field hospital.
They could not transport me to the regular hospital.
They didn't think I'd make it.
I floated between life and death.
They eventually transferred to me to the regular hospital on the grounds of Bergen-Belsen.
It's a big hospital-- put me on the death ward.
I know that, because after I didn't die and got stronger, I used to help the nurses-- 37 beds.
The turnover was probably every hour.
That was the death rate.
Every time I'd open my eyes, there would be a different face on each side of me.
They were narrow, little hospital beds with the railings up.
I'd look through the railing and see somebody else unconscious.
And then I opened my eyes, and lo and behold, there were bright blue eyes looking at me.
We smiled.
I passed out.
Came to-- I was scared to turn over and see.
But I did-- same eyes, the same girl.
We started a conversation in between my passing out.
She wasn't nearly as bad as I was.
Her name was Zasha.
She was a Polish Catholic-- 21.
She had been caught in the Warsaw massacre.
She saw her mother raped to death; the square running with human blood.
Her father was killed.
Her husband was shipped off to a labor camp someplace.
They had a one-year-old baby boy who was dismembered in front of their eyes-- just ripped-- limb by limb.
And she was grabbed and shipped off to a labor camp.
Somehow or other, Polish Catholic Zasha and Jewish Miriam, who was [NON-ENGLISH] those days, were both liberated in Bergen-Belsen.
